learning_objectives: Distinguish between positivist, interpretivist, and pragmatist research paradigms
Identify research questions appropriate for quantitative, qualitative, and mixed methods
Evaluate the quality of published educational technology research
readings: Reeves, T. - Design-Based Research in Educational Technology

lecture_notes.introduction: Research in educational technology draws from education, psychology, computer science, and design. Understanding different research paradigms and methods equips you to both consume research critically and produce it rigorously.
key_concepts: Positivism: measurable outcomes, controlled experiments, generalizability
Interpretivism: understanding meaning, context, lived experience
Pragmatism: choosing methods based on the research question
Design-based research: iterating interventions in authentic contexts
The replication crisis and its implications for ed tech research
lecture_notes.demonstration: Research question workshop: transform three vague ed tech questions into well-formed research questions, identifying the appropriate paradigm and method for each
setup_requirements: Published ed tech studies for analysis

review.value: Uniquely suited to educational technology: researchers and practitioners collaborate to iteratively design, implement, and refine interventions in authentic settings, generating both practical solutions and theoretical understanding.
Many published ed tech studies have small samples, no control group, and inflated effect sizes. Consumers of research must evaluate methodology, not just conclusions.

learning_objectives: Design randomized controlled trials for educational interventions
Identify threats to internal and external validity
Apply quasi-experimental designs when randomization is not possible
readings: Shadish, Cook & Campbell - Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs Ch. 1-3
What Works Clearinghouse: Standards for evaluating educational research

lecture_notes.introduction: The gold standard for causal inference is the randomized controlled trial. In education, true randomization is often difficult, making quasi-experimental designs essential tools in the researcher's toolkit.
key_concepts: RCT design: random assignment, treatment/control, pre/post measurement
Threats to internal validity: history, maturation, testing, selection bias
Threats to external validity: sample representativeness, setting, treatment fidelity
Quasi-experimental designs: pre-post with comparison group, interrupted time series, regression discontinuity
Effect size calculation and interpretation: Cohen's d, practical significance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design critique exercise: evaluate the methodology of three published ed tech RCTs, identifying strengths, validity threats, and practical significance
setup_requirements: Three published RCTs in educational technology

review.value: In ed tech studies, the biggest threats are selection bias (who opts into the technology), novelty effect (excitement about new technology), and treatment fidelity (was the technology used as designed?).
Statistical significance (p < .05) does not mean the effect matters. A Cohen's d of 0.2 is statistically significant with a large sample but may not justify the cost of the intervention.

learning_objectives: Design qualitative studies using case study, ethnography, and phenomenology approaches
Conduct effective research interviews and observations
Apply qualitative coding and thematic analysis techniques
readings: Saldaña, J. - The Coding Manual for Qualitative Researchers Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Quantitative methods tell us whether something works. Qualitative methods tell us how and why. In educational technology, understanding the human experience of using technology is essential for designing better interventions.
key_concepts: Case study: in-depth investigation of a bounded system
Ethnography: immersive observation of technology use in cultural context
Phenomenology: understanding the lived experience of learners using technology
Data collection: semi-structured interviews, observation, document analysis, artifacts
Coding: open, axial, selective; thematic analysis process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Coding practice: given a transcript of a student interview about online learning, apply open coding to identify initial themes, then organize themes into categories

review.value: Qualitative research uses trustworthiness criteria (credibility, transferability, dependability, confirmability) rather than validity/reliability. Member checking, triangulation, and thick description are key strategies.
Data collection continues until new interviews/observations no longer generate new themes. This is not a fixed number of participants but a qualitative judgment.

learning_objectives: Design survey instruments with attention to validity and reliability
Apply Likert scale and other response format best practices
Analyze survey data using descriptive and inferential statistics
readings: Dillman, D. - Internet, Phone, Mail, and Mixed-Mode Surveys Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Surveys are the most commonly used research instrument in educational technology, and the most commonly done poorly. This session covers the science of asking questions well.
key_concepts: Item writing: clarity, single-barreled questions, appropriate vocabulary
Response formats: Likert scales, semantic differential, ranking, open-ended
Common errors: leading questions, double-barreled, acquiescence bias, social desirability
Pilot testing: cognitive interviews and item revision
Analysis: reliability (Cronbach's alpha), factor analysis, descriptive and inferential statistics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Survey revision workshop: critique and rewrite 10 poorly designed survey items, then test revised items through cognitive interviews with a partner

review.value: Questions that ask about two things at once ('How satisfied are you with the content and navigation of the course?') are unanswerable because the respondent may feel differently about each.
Measures internal consistency of a scale. Alpha > 0.7 is generally acceptable. Below 0.7 suggests items may not be measuring the same construct.

learning_objectives: Design mixed methods studies that integrate quantitative and qualitative data
Apply design-based research methodology to educational technology development
Evaluate when each methodology is most appropriate
readings: Creswell, J. - A Concise Introduction to Mixed Methods Research
McKenney, S. & Reeves, T. - Conducting Educational Design Research Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: The most impactful educational technology research often combines methods. Mixed methods integrates quantitative measurement with qualitative understanding. Design-based research embeds research in the iterative design process itself.
key_concepts: Mixed methods designs: convergent, explanatory sequential, exploratory sequential
Integration strategies: merging, connecting, embedding
Design-based research cycles: analysis, design, development, implementation, evaluation, redesign
DBR outputs: both practical (improved intervention) and theoretical (design principles)
Balancing rigor and relevance in applied research
lecture_notes.demonstration: DBR planning exercise: design a 3-cycle design-based research study for improving an online course, specifying research questions, data collection, and design iterations for each cycle

review.value: The power of mixed methods is in the integration, not in doing both separately. The quantitative and qualitative strands must be connected at the design, collection, analysis, or interpretation stage.
DBR produces generalizable design principles ('When designing X for audience Y in context Z, doing A produces better outcomes than doing B') that transfer beyond the specific study context.

class_schedule.topic: Research Ethics and Writing for Publication
learning_objectives: Apply IRB requirements to educational technology research
Write research proposals and manuscripts for academic publication
Navigate the peer review and publication process
readings: APA - Ethical Principles in Research with Human Participants
Sword, H. - Stylish Academic Writing Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethical research protects participants. Effective writing communicates findings to the field. This session covers both the regulatory requirements and the craft of scholarly communication.
key_concepts: IRB review: exempt, expedited, full board categories
Informed consent in educational research: when is it needed?
Student data and FERPA: research vs. program evaluation
Manuscript structure: IMRaD (Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion)
The peer review process: submission, review, revision, decision
lecture_notes.demonstration: Proposal writing exercise: draft the methods section of a research proposal including participants, design, instruments, procedure, and data analysis plan

review.value: Exempt: minimal risk, normal educational practices, anonymous surveys. Expedited: identifiable data but minimal risk. Full board: vulnerable populations, deception, or above-minimal risk.
The best research is useless if it is unreadable. Lead with the problem, not the methodology. Write for the practitioner who will use your findings, not just for other researchers.

quizzes.topic: Research Paradigms, Experimental Design, and Qualitative Methods

questions.question: A researcher finds that students using an AI tutor score 8% higher on a post-test than a control group (p = .03, d = 0.25). The sample is 40 students from a single university. Evaluate this finding: is it strong evidence that the AI tutor is effective? Identify two validity threats.
questions.answer: This is weak evidence despite statistical significance. The effect size (d = 0.25) is small and may not justify the cost and effort of implementing the AI tutor. Two validity threats: (1) External validity: a single university with 40 students limits generalizability to other populations and settings. (2) Novelty effect: students may have been more engaged with the AI tutor because it was new, not because it was pedagogically superior. A delayed post-test would help distinguish novelty from genuine learning. Additional concern: with n=40, the study is underpowered and the significant p-value may not replicate.

rubric.key: Assesses practical significance using effect size
Identifies two specific validity threats with explanation
Notes limitations of small sample size

questions.question: When would you choose qualitative methods over quantitative methods for studying an educational technology intervention? Give a specific scenario and justify your choice.
questions.answer: Choose qualitative methods when you need to understand HOW and WHY an intervention works (or fails), not just WHETHER it works. Scenario: A school has implemented a collaborative learning platform, and quantitative data shows no improvement in test scores. However, teachers report that students seem more engaged and collaborative. A qualitative case study with classroom observations, student interviews, and artifact analysis can reveal: what types of collaboration are occurring, how students experience the platform, what barriers prevent deeper engagement, and whether the platform is developing skills (collaboration, communication) not captured by standardized tests. This understanding informs redesign in ways that a larger quantitative study cannot.

rubric.key: Articulates when qualitative methods are appropriate
Explains what qualitative methods reveal that quantitative cannot

quizzes.topic: Surveys, Mixed Methods, and Research Ethics

questions.question: Identify three common errors in survey design and explain how each affects data quality. Rewrite one example of a flawed item.
questions.answer: (1) Double-barreled questions ('How satisfied are you with the course content and instructor?') — respondents may feel differently about each, making the response uninterpretable. (2) Leading questions ('Don't you agree that technology improves learning?') — wording biases respondents toward a particular answer, inflating positive responses. (3) Vague response scales ('How often do you use technology? Rarely / Sometimes / Often') — without anchors, 'sometimes' means different things to different people. Rewrite of #1: Split into two items: 'How satisfied are you with the course content?' and 'How satisfied are you with the instructor?' each with the same 5-point scale.

rubric.key: Identifies three distinct survey design errors
Explains how each affects data quality
Provides a correct rewrite of a flawed item

questions.question: What is design-based research and how does it differ from a traditional experimental study? Identify one advantage and one limitation of DBR.
questions.answer: Design-based research is an iterative methodology where researchers and practitioners collaborate to design, implement, evaluate, and refine an intervention in an authentic educational setting through multiple cycles. Unlike traditional experiments, DBR: (1) does not hold the intervention constant — it deliberately improves it across iterations, (2) occurs in real classrooms, not controlled labs, and (3) produces both practical outputs (improved intervention) and theoretical outputs (design principles). Advantage: DBR generates solutions that work in real contexts because they are developed and tested there. Traditional experiments may show efficacy in controlled settings that does not transfer. Limitation: because the intervention changes across cycles, it is difficult to make strong causal claims about which specific design features produced the observed outcomes.

rubric.key: Clear distinction from traditional experimental studies
Identifies both an advantage and a limitation

major_assignments.title: Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Write a complete research proposal for a study investigating an educational technology question. The proposal must include: an introduction with literature review, clearly stated research questions, a detailed methods section (design, participants, instruments, procedure, data analysis), an ethical considerations section, and a timeline. The proposal should be formatted for submission to an educational technology journal.
objectives: Formulate research questions grounded in the literature
Design a rigorous study appropriate to the research questions
Write a professional research proposal in academic format
Address ethical considerations for human subjects research
deliverables: Research proposal (4,000-5,000 words in APA format)
Instruments appendix (survey, interview protocol, or observation rubric)

criteria: Clear problem statement grounded in the literature
Comprehensive literature review identifying the gap
Research questions logically derived from the review
Appropriate design for the research questions
Detailed participant description and sampling strategy
Valid instruments with evidence of reliability
Rigorous methodology appropriate to the questions

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on research methods concepts

lab_assignments.description: Weekly methods practice: survey design, coding, statistical analysis, literature review

midterm_examination.description: Covers research paradigms, experimental design, and qualitative methods

final_project.description: Pilot study: implement one component of the research proposal and report findings

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected. Methods workshops and peer review sessions require participation. More than 3 absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Literature review and methods drafts must be submitted on time for peer feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review of research proposals is required. All proposals must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Proper citation of all sources is required. Fabrication of data results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with internet access. Reference management software (Zotero or Mendeley). Spreadsheet or statistical software for data analysis practice.
